content:
🚨 IMPORTANT: My LinkedIn account was restricted for 48 hours. 
Let's go through these points so you can avoid the same little drama 👇 

I lost access to my account and couldn't reach support. 

BUT 

I managed to get partial help from the LinkedIn Help account on X via DMs. 
They provided me with a form to submit my case and create a support ticket. 

❓ So, what were the reasons? 

According to their response, these were the triggers: 

• 𝗠𝘆 𝗮𝗰𝗰𝗼𝘂𝗻𝘁 𝗵𝗮𝘀 𝗮 𝗵𝘂𝗴𝗲 𝗮𝗺𝗼𝘂𝗻𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗶𝘁𝘆 
– They suspected automation, but I engage manually. 
– Solution: Check your browser plugins and extensions and the access you gave to certain tools. 
– Navigation: Settings & Privacy → Data Privacy → Manage Data Permissions. 

• 𝗠𝘆 𝗮𝗰𝗰𝗼𝘂𝗻𝘁 𝗶𝘀 𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗳𝗶𝗲𝗱, 𝗯𝘂𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗻𝗮𝗺𝗲 𝗼𝗻 𝗺𝘆 𝗜𝗗 𝗱𝗶𝗱𝗻'𝘁 𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗰𝗵 𝗺𝘆 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗳𝗶𝗹𝗲 𝗻𝗮𝗺𝗲 
– Solution: I had to remove my unicorn emoji and switch from my nickname to my official name. 🦄 Nika Kotláriková → Nikola Kotláriková 

• 𝗠𝘂𝗹𝘁𝗶𝗽𝗹𝗲 𝗮𝗰𝗰𝗼𝘂𝗻𝘁𝘀 
– I have a backup account and was asked to either merge or close it. 
– Solution: I had to list the emails and profile links of accounts I wanted to remove. 

There's still a chance my account could be banned completely. 
I'm doing my best to engage with you all, but... rules are rules. 

For now:
– I'll be moderating my activity a little. 
– If you'd like to stay in touch regardless, feel free to drop me a DM with your email, just so we don't lose each other. 

P.S. 1: LinkedIn support was actually really collegial. 
P.S. 2: The 48-hour ban had one benefit (I got to enjoy a forced digital detox). P.S. 3: I used that free time meaninglessly. By wasting it on X. 

But so happy to be back. :D

TL;DR: LinkedIn is bul*ying me, but I'm still finding my way to be with you.

content:
LinkedIn account bans, content building blocks, and why being yourself on sales calls might actually be hurting you.

3 fav posts on LinkedIn this week 💙

1️⃣ Richard Moore
Richard made a point that most founders get wrong on sales calls, and it's the idea that "being yourself" is always a good thing.

Because when you're uncertain about your offer, being yourself just means:
→ showing up as the most apologetic version of yourself
→ softening your price
→ rushing through the awkward bit
→ and making your offer sound smaller than it actually is, which causes buyers to treat it exactly that way.

Read here what to do instead:
https://lnkd.in/emgPaZBY

2️⃣ Filipa Canelas
Filipa shared a simple system for creating LinkedIn content without burning yourself out every week.

The idea is that once you know which opening combination fits each type of post, whether it's a framework, a case study, or a data breakdown, you can build a library of reusable content blocks that you package and serve based on where your audience is in their journey.

Read it here: https://lnkd.in/e3jYkJg6

3️⃣ Nikola Kotláriková
Nikola opened up about getting her LinkedIn account restricted for 48 hours and broke down the three things that triggered it.

Which were:
→ high account activity that looked automated
→ a mismatch between her verified ID name and her profile display name
→ and having a backup account that LinkedIn flagged as a policy violation

So if you're active on the platform, it's worth auditing your third-party tool permissions and making sure everything on your profile lines up with your official information before LinkedIn does it for you.

Read it here: https://lnkd.in/eEJdvjgW

Hope these add value to your week. Let me know which one resonated most with you.

PS. Every Friday I do a round-up of my 3 fav posts on LinkedIn that I find in my Postiv.ai inspi library. Follow me to stay in the loop!

content:
6 months ago, I spent 2 days on a hackathon deck. Last week, I discovered a tool that could do it in 30 min. 🤦‍♀️ Nwm. It can all be done in 6 simple steps: 

1. Connect the Gamma app to Claude. 
2. Brainstorm the presentation structure and text with Claude. 
3. Ask Gamma to apply the text to the presentation. (No need to leave a dashboard.) 
4. Optionally, extract information from Notion / Gmail – I keep my ideas in email.
5. Ask Claude to summarise those texts from Gmail. 
6. Ask Gamma to update the presentation with the new data. 

Guys... 

This tool was released a few weeks ago. 

If it had existed half a year ago, I could have slept like a normal human being instead of messing with text alignment, photos, and formatting during the night. 

At least now I know this option exists, so next time I’ll save time and nerves. 

BTW, with the help of Gamma, I created a presentation on:

👉 𝗛𝗼𝘄 𝘁𝗼 𝗴𝗲𝘁 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗳𝗶𝗿𝘀𝘁 𝟭𝗞 𝗟𝗶𝗻𝗸𝗲𝗱𝗜𝗻 𝗳𝗼𝗹𝗹𝗼𝘄𝗲𝗿𝘀

content:
LinkedIn: Engage more to grow your network. 
Also LinkedIn: We restricted your account for engaging too much. 💀 

This has been haunting me for 3 months straight, and I don't use any automation tools. I do everything manually. 

LinkedIn can penalise you for almost anything: 
– Engaging too much 
– Using the 3rd party tools 
– Viewing too many profiles 
– Sending too many messages 
– Sending too many connection requests, etc. 

Even Premium users aren't safe. 

I can't imagine this would happen to my clients who have put real effort, time, and money into building their presence here. 

It's demotivating. 

Until LinkedIn fixes this, the best you can do is: 
→ Stay moderate / controlled with your activity 
→ Keep a backup account 
→ Build an email list 
→ Avoid the 3rd party tools
→ Connect with someone on LinkedIn's internal team 

TL;DR: You don't own this platform or your audience. Don't rely on it blindly.

content:
Yesterday, I almost didn't post something that had 8,6k+ impressions. 
I didn't think it would take off just because: 

– I'd already seen it done by someone else 
– It felt like a repeated joke, a "copy-paste" 
– It didn't feel deep or meaningful to me 
– It had no image to catch anyone's eye 

And yet... 

The post performed well: 

• 8,600+ impressions 
• 222 reactions 
• 411 comments 
• 61 profile views 
• 27 new connections 

So... guys, if you are afraid of, press that "Enter" button anyway. 

– People forget who posted it first (but give credit regardless) 
– People love to laugh and be part of a joke 
– You don't always need to be serious 
– Short text can perform just as well as image + text 

And if it flops, don't worry. 
Nobody will notice except you. 

You'll post something better next time. 

Sometimes all you need [besides a good idea] is 

👉 A little more trust in yourself.
…more

content:
People say they don't know what to write about, but they underestimate the biggest source of inspiration: 

Their own life. 

Everyone is trying to impress with a high MRR or generate text with AI. 

It's tiring. 

We want authenticity! 

– Share your experiences. 
– Share your fu*k ups. 
– Share your lessons learned. 

We kind of know that life isn't perfect, so we forgive imperfection. 

Mistakes exist because they happen in life. 

So don't be shy about sharing them. 
Make fun of it sometimes. 

And, who knows... 🤷‍♀️ 

Maybe that very post will blow up. 

TL;DR: Life is imperfectly beautiful. Share it.

content:
I haven’t ranted for two whole days. Let’s fix that. 😅 
This time, there’s something we could do much better: 

👉 Please avoid jumping on calls in your very first message. 👈 

I know you want money (we all do), but the way it’s often executed feels intrusive. 

You wouldn’t propose to someone on the first date. 

You need to get to know each other first. 
Selling is about building relationships with care – it’s a long process. 

And that’s okay. 

But there’s one thing that makes it faster: 

MARKETING → The reputation you build for yourself. 

If you build a personal brand, it becomes much easier to attract clients. 

It’s no longer just you pushing from your side. 

People start coming to you. 

Since I started posting regularly on LinkedIn, 
people have been reaching out to me in DMs. 

Not the other way around. 

– I share something valuable. 
– They find it interesting. 
– They reach out. 
– And that’s where I get my opportunity. 

Marketing + Sales = 🤝 (go hand in hand) 

Remember that.

content:
For the mother of God, please post on LinkedIn. 
Please hear me out, you need to read this: 

I started posting consistently 6 months ago. 

Just one post per day. 

Since then, I've: 
– gained 4,000+ followers 
– booked 5 sponsors 
– landed a client 
– received 4 – 5 additional collaboration offers 
– been invited to speak at a conference 
– been approached to deliver 2 company trainings 
– and there’s still more coming in 

If you feel awkward about posting and decide not to, you’ll never get feedback, and you’ll never create opportunities for yourself. 

F*ck shyness. 

Life is too short to be serious and afraid of things that might never even happen. 

Just write something about the topic you like and press "Enter." 

Don't overthink it. 

Act! 

🫳🎤

content:
LinkedIn is changing, and I don't mean it's becoming cringe. 
There's something much bigger behind it: 

– The platform jumped from 1.2B → 1.3B members in just 5 months. 
– LinkedIn Premium revenue grew from $1.7B → $2B in under a year (24 – 25). 
– 175 million Premium subscribers projected by 2026. 

Do you know what that means? 

LINKEDIN EXPECTS GROWTH! 

That's why so many ads. 

More members = More opportunities = More sales. (Or "Jump on calls"?) 😰 

But with the good news comes the bad: 
• more competition 
• people screaming for attention 
• AI slop everywhere 

I believe we can take the platform back to something better. 

And you're just in time to get the most out of it. 

START BUILDING A PERSONAL BRAND: 
→ Show your face. 
→ Share your own experience = authenticity. 
→ Jump on friendly coffee chats to prove you're a real human. 

Life on LinkedIn can be good. 

But only if each of us does our part. 

Enjoy the ride. 

✌️

Two LinkedIn formats perform better than others on this platform. They are: 


1. Image + Text 
2. Text 

Naïlé Titah also mentioned this in his mini-study. (sharing in comments) 

This goes against everything LinkedIn often says: “Post videos and carousels.” 

Yes, that would be good for LinkedIn in terms of time spent on posts and on the platform itself. 

But users are responding to something else. 

Regardless of format, what matters even more is: 
→ What topic you write about 
→ How interestingly you present it 
→ Who posts it (authority, celebrity, etc.) 
→ How much you interact with people 

Focus on this as well. 

P.S. Both posts in the image were text-based and received solid attention. There might be some truth in that. 🙂

→ Be funny or speak from your own experience. 
→ And yes, you can use AI, but ideally only to polish your grammar. I use it too.

Back to work. (And meaningful commenting.)

Yesterday’s post about massive growth on LinkedIn brought me 20 new connections and 50+ profile visits. I’m not surprised at all why: 

People want to know the secret formula for growth. 
Success attracts attention. 

It’s simple:

– And a potential collaboration (a sponsor). 

When you help others grow, you grow too. 

I will be posting more content about LinkedIn on this profile. 

If you want to understand personal brand growth...

content:
I noticed one pattern among people who grew massively on LinkedIn. 
What is that? 

Their massive growth started when one of their posts went viral. 

For example, Jasmine Gathoga had a viral post in October 2025 (~9k followers), and since then gained a large number of new followers (~24k). 

I have seen this pattern among many creators on LinkedIn as well as on X. 

Having a post go viral encourages creators to: 
– analyse it 
– experiment with topics 
– spend more time posting and staying active on the platform 

In other words: 
The effort of posting and the potential results accumulate over time. 
The more intentionally you post, the higher your chances of breaking through. 

I also had one post go viral in September 2025, which motivated me to post more frequently and consistently. 

3 weeks ago, I had another post go viral. 

Overall, I grew in impressions, engagement, and followers. 

→ Keep posting. 
→ Keep interacting. 
→ That more viral post will come. 

And most importantly, don’t get discouraged. 
(This is also the TL;DR of this post.)

content:
How to start posting when you’re just getting started on LinkedIn? 

Definitely try these 10 things: 

1. Research creators with high engagement. 

2. Analyse their content. 

3. Think about how you could reuse their structure for your industry or case with slight adjustments. 

4. Ask ChatGPT what works and why it works that way – look for the reasoning behind it. 

5. If possible, prepare 6 – 7 pieces of content in one day (so you have the whole week planned). 

6. Ask ChatGPT for recommendations on how to improve your drafts. 

7. After posting, engage with people for at least the first 60 minutes – reply to as many comments as you can. 

8. Return to the post the next day – reply again and consider reposting it. 

9. At the end of the week, analyse which content performed best. You can reuse the format later with a different topic or angle. 

10. Repeat. 

This is what I outlined in my communication with Mamesi Lamoen, and I believe it’s worth sharing publicly, because everyone deserves a more structured and consistent feed. 

------

content:
It’s going to sound a bit toxic, but I need to say it aloud: 


I hate 9 – 5 in-office work, especially in the creative industry. 

You are not creative from 9 to 5. 
That’s simply not how creativity works. 

I remember being an intern a few years ago at one of the best advertising agencies in my country, and they had a very strict in-office schedule. 

After sitting there for 8 hours, locked between four walls, I didn’t come up with a single usable idea. 😀 

The ideas came later: 
– Around 11 PM in the shower 
– During weekend walks in the park 
– When I was sitting at home on the toilet, just staring into nothing 

I felt it was a waste of my time + freedom, as I had to stay in one place for 8 hrs, while my work often spilled into my free time as ideas came outside the office. 

Yesterday, I also read a post by Kilian Markert. 

The TL;DR of that post was that the problem is not that “remote work is inefficient,” but rather about: 
– WHO you hire, 
– HOW you manage them, and 
– HOW you track progress + time. 

This applies to the creative industry, too. 

Don’t lock creatives in a cage. 

Give them the freedom to explore the world and new ideas. 

It will benefit both them and you. 

Just make sure you have the fundamentals that Kilian mentioned in his post, and you will still get the creative output you need. 

I think I said everything I wanted to say. 

[P.S. If it were up to me and I had the budget, I would send creatives to travel the world, let them absorb experiences like a sponge, and then go wild in their creation.] 🙂
…more

content:
This post drew me 107 profile visits. 0 effort for graphic design. Just 2 sentences. Why did it work so well? 👇 

Because of 2 things: 

1) I used a native LinkedIn celebratory graphic design 

Work anniversaries, job changes, and promotions are platform-generated milestones. 

Historically, these posts: 
– Generate high engagement rates 
– Trigger notifications to connections 
– Encourage congratulatory comments 

So LinkedIn already has strong performance data on them. 

When something consistently performs well, the algorithm becomes more confident in distributing it more widely during early testing. 

2) I interrupted the pattern 

AKA turned a well-known celebratory post into a cringe MEME. 

The post brought: 
– 11,514 Impressions 
– 463 reactions 
– 213 comments 
– 22 followers 
– 5 saves 
– 3 reposts 
– 3 resends 

You know the saying: work smart, not hard. 

So this is the execution. 😅

content:
I’ll be honest with you guys (because I think you deserve to know the truth). Most of my posts don’t perform very well. BUT! ... 

I take steps to make them look "popular." 

No, I don’t run ads. 

It’s a more time-consuming problem. 

If you want a post to look "good," you have to contribute to it. 

Specifically: 
1. Engage with your own post (or respond from your page account) 
2. Leave the first comment 
3. Repost it after some time 
4. Comment as much as possible within the replies 
5. Tag relevant people who might be interested or add value 
6. Encourage your team or close colleagues to react first 
7. Share the post in relevant private groups or communities 

(I personally practice the first four points.) 

This isn’t some big secret. 

A lot of people know this, but still don’t do it. 🤷‍♀️ 

I just thought it was worth reminding, since many of you ask me about miracles. 

There are no miracles. 

Only the steps you need to make time for and actually do. 

If you want a post to live, you have to bring it to life yourself. That's it.

content:
If you want to play it safe, there are specific topics that are likely to get attention on this platform. They are: 

– talking about how to build & grow LinkedIn in your niche 
– revealing the “top secret” recipe behind your first $1M+ 
– sharing a success or milestone (e.g., how you went viral) 
– posting an image or text-based meme 
– announcing that you’re hiring [it's a career platform, indeed]

BTW: 

Once you hit one viral post, it gets easier. 

Because… 

1. One viral post gives you more content – you can analyse it, break down the results, share lessons learned, etc. 

2. It transfers attention from the viral post to your other posts (if you leverage it while it’s still fresh and people remember you). 

3. The initial euphoria motivates you to create more – and you naturally put more effort into your content. 

But you know... my general suggestion is: 

Experiment. 

Over time, things can change. 

Happy weekend!

content:
100k impressions, 1k likes, and 400 comments in 24 hours for a particular post. 
What was the magic sauce that made my content go viral? 👇

I see two main things that made it happen: 

1) A LinkedIn-centric meme that was funny, with an image from a cult series. 
2) It was accompanied by an educational text about where LinkedIn is heading. 

When you want to get attention and scale a post, you need to: 
– use a catchy visual 
– talk about a topic that is relevant to the platform (LinkedIn) 
– have a strong hook 
– structure the post for readability 
– engage actively 

(I spent ~ 6 hours on LinkedIn engaging and commenting around this post.) 😀 

Remember: 

If people like a specific social media platform, they want to dominate it. 

If they want to dominate it, they want to know the latest updates + predictions. 

That’s why they are curious about such posts. 

TL;DR: Mix well-established visuals with a topic relevant to the target audience and the platform. 

P.S. I am running a survey about LinkedIn behaviour (usage + consumption). 

If you want to be part of my weird experiments, feel free to participate. 

Link in the comments.

content:
“LinkedIn is de*d on weekends. It’s not worth posting.” 

Pche. Please. My stats say something else. Transparent breakdown: 👇 

During Saturday and Sunday, I gained more impressions and new connections than I usually do during an entire workweek (sometimes even a whole month). 

Specifically, in total: 
– 112,971 impressions 
– 1,446 likes 
– 688 comments 
– 548 profile visits 
– 110 new connections 
– 85 saves 
– 22 sends 
– 16 reposts 

It’s not about whether people are here or not (they are). 
It’s about how interestingly you shape and communicate your idea. 

On both days, I used MEMEs: 
→ One was text-only (4 lines). 
→ The other was an image + a longer caption. 
(The image was a funny, LinkedIn-centric MEME. The text was educational.) 

On the one hand, people say weekends are weaker. 
On the other hand, people finally have time to scroll. 

Don’t underestimate weekends. 
Just don't! 

It might be a missed opportunity. 

I’ll come back to this post later and share a proper breakdown. 

Stay tuned (and follow along).

content:
Having 1k followers on LinkedIn is not a flex anymore. Things have changed. 👇

The platform jumped from 1.2B → 1.3B members in ~5 months. 

More users = More content = More noise = Harder to differentiate 

LinkedIn is "mainstreaming." 

Content gets simplified. 

From Professional & Educational. 
To Cringe & Situational. 

Also, the follower count is not the only important metric. 

Engagement > Followers 

Sponsors want to know about: 
– Impressions 
– Engagement 
– Audience structure
– CTRs 
– CPM 

If you want to grow, earn attention before you teach.

You can still be educational, but in a different way.

Education still works, but packaged as infotainment with insights. 

Everybody on the platform... 

Normally, I’d say “Good luck.”

But in this case: 

Observe, experiment, and adjust. 

2026 will be crazy for us!

content:
Which content is better: Short or Long? And how not to be boring or too educational? Well, let me explain: 

Our attention span is so poor that reading a single line of text feels like a victory. 

So not gonna lie – we’re doomed. 😂 

Anyway... 

Long-form content only works if: 
– You’re a major authority, so people pay full attention out of respect. 
– You’re a celebrity, and your audience hangs on your every word. 
– The topic hits their pain point directly. 
– You’re expressing strong emotions (anger, disappointment, personal story). 

Otherwise, SHORTER IS BETTER. 

Right now, single-line jokes and quick, punchy posts are winning on LinkedIn. 

✅ How to make your content educational without being boring: 
– Use simple, clear language (avoid jargon).
– Add humour or a fun concept (MEMEs help!). 
– Break text into paragraphs for readability. 
– Skip long elaborations – save deep dives for a blog. 
– Be a little messy sometimes – perfection is boring. 

These 2 topics are very similar, so I decided to merge them into 1. 

Thanks, Vaibhav Lodaya, for the topic idea. 

And if you think that this information can help someone, just... 

🔁 Repost

content:
Why do creators post the first comment under their own posts?
There are at least 3 reasons. (+ My personal approach) 👇 

I see three main reasons why people publish the first comment under their own post: 

1. You build a habit in your followers, nudging the algorithm in your favour 

A smart first comment trains your audience to check the comments, boosting engagement and making your post more noticeable. 

→ LinkedIn interprets this as extra positive activity: people clicking into comments, interacting with your comment, and spending time in the discussion. 

2. Build a trusted space for sharing links 

→ You are building an audience that will potentially see your link to the product or service when you post it in the comments in the future. 

3. Increase post engagement on your own 

→ This activity boosts your post’s metrics even before anyone else engages. Psychologically, it signals that someone has already interacted with your content, making it appear more interesting. 

👉 How do I personally approach it? 
– I see the internet as a place for fun. That’s why I use memes. 
– Sometimes the comment section is more entertaining than the post itself. 
– I want people to associate my profile with something "enjoyable." 
– Memes work best for me + clever text jokes. 
– Ideally, when it is short. 

By the way, thanks to Cédric Berry-Croguennec and others for your questions on yesterday’s post. They’ll inspire future content on specific topics. 

TL;DR: Don't forget to write the 1st comment. ✌️

content:
What really matters in a LinkedIn post – do not neglect the last part tho: 
(ranked by impact – descending order) 

1. The topic + timing (if it’s not relevant, you lose the reader) 
2. Format 
3. Compelling visual (it stops the scroll) 
4. The first 1–2 lines of text ~150 characters (hook) 
5. Content structure (paragraphs, spacing, readability) 
6. Clear takeaway 
7. Strong CTA 
8. First comment to stimulate conversation 

Of course, the order of importance can change if you try a different type of post, like text-only. In that case, the topic and hook are equally important. 

I want to create more posts like this on marketing. 

If there’s a specific topic you’d like me to cover, leave me a food for thought in a comment! 

I’ll try to break it down next time.

content:
Find people who will support you in the comments, even when your post flops. 
I’ll be honest with you (transparency above all): 

Over the past 6 months of consistently posting on LinkedIn, most of my posts failed. 

Yet thanks to these people, some of my posts did well – especially in terms of engagement in the comments. 

Here’s what I do: 

– I actively engage with these people in the comments. 
– They comment. → I reply. 
– They see that I don’t ignore their comments. 
– They realise their energy isn’t wasted. 
– They remember me. ↔ I remember them. 
– They come back and support me AGAIN. 

(A human relationship can’t be built by any AI model.) 

You can’t rely on people showing up for you only when your posts are good. Most of your posts will flop.

𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗵𝗮𝘃𝗲 𝘁𝗼 𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗹𝘆 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝗿𝗲𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽𝘀 𝘀𝗼 𝗽𝗲𝗼𝗽𝗹𝗲 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗻 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗽𝗼𝘀𝘁𝘀 𝗮𝗿𝗲𝗻’𝘁 𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗴 – 𝘄𝗵𝗶𝗰𝗵 𝗶𝘀 𝗺𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲. 

I’m not the most consistent at replying in DMs, but I remember who does what in their free time. I care. 

TL;DR: Build relationships.
…more

content:
My post from yesterday almost flopped. It didn’t look promising at first – but I managed to turn it around using one method. What happened? 

I forced attention. 

It’s well known that Discussion > Passive engagement. 

Writing a comment keeps people under a post much longer than simply hitting the Like button.

I posted on Sunday evening CET.

• In the first 30 minutes: 5 likes, 9 comments, 99 impressions. 
Well below my average. 

• In the first hour: 8 likes, ~24 comments, ~250 impressions. 
I kept actively leading the conversation in the comments. 

• After 16 hours: 
41 likes, 134 comments, ~1,522 impressions, 4 profile visits. 

Nothing is lost if you put effort into scaling your post. 

Every one of your posts should receive extra care. 
Even those not promising.

content:
WHAT IS THE BEST TIME FOR POSTING ON LINKEDIN?
👇👇👇

If you’re trying to reach audiences across continents, it gets tricky. 

⚠️ THERE’S NO PERFECT TIME THAT WORKS FOR EVERYONE. 

For example, posting in the morning CET works best for me. 

→ When I post in the morning, it hits active audiences in Western Europe → the post gets early reactions, comments, and dwell time. 
→ The algorithm sees this and thinks: “OK, this is interesting.” 
→ When the US wakes up (my afternoon CET), the post’s early engagement helps it rank higher than fresh posts. 

So I can catch more waves this way. 

----------

P.S. 

People keep asking me why I’ve been posting content in the evenings lately. Whether it’s some kind of strategy to reach markets in the US. The truth is: 

It's not an extra tactic.

I just don't have time to post early in the morning. 😄 

Happy weekend, guys!

Author of Super Skill: Why Storytelling Is the Superpower of the AI Age (An Amazon #1 Release!) & The Storytelling Edge | Frac. CMO @ Pepper, Mastering AI Search | Keynote Speaker on Storytelling & AI
content:
Another big publication confirms it: Storytelling is the hottest business skill of the AI Age.

There are a few reasons we’re seeing this trend grow—and why it’s different than the “brand journalism” wave of the 2010s. 

1. AI Slop is a forcing function. Brands used to get away with churning out bland, inoffensive content. But AI has driven the cost of creating mediocre content down to zero. To have any chance of penetrating through the slop, you need to create truly exceptional content. Contrary to popular belief, AI has made great storytellers MORE valuable.

2. You can’t buy attention anymore. You have to earn it. That means developing enduring IP and entertainment that wins over customers. This is why companies like GAP are hiring Chief Entertainment Officers to develop original shows. (And why more B2B founders should develop unique, contrarian IP and write books.)

3. Storytelling is now a CEO-level initiative. This is the most important shift. Ten years ago, storytelling and content initiatives were confined to a small, isolated team locked in the basement of the marketing department. Today, it’s a top, CEO-level priority. 

The most valuable storytellers are those who work hand in hand with the CEO to craft the narrative and then build a storytelling system that instills it across the organization. 

Great storytellers who can manage change and build scalable systems have never been more valuable. 

I’ve spent the past three years researching and reporting on this shift, and creating the playbook for mastering storytelling in the AI Age. Check out my new book Super Skill: Why Storytelling Is the Superpower of the AI Age and unlock bonus chapters, free courses, workshops, and more: https://lnkd.in/g2M-3cSs

content:
Sorry, not so sorry, but today I’m gonna be a little harsh: 
Your posts aren’t as good or as important as you think. Rant continues: 

If you’re overthinking every detail to make your content perfect, you might just be wasting… time, energy, and mood. 

Let’s be honest: 

Your post isn’t going to bring world peace or cure cancer. 

If it flops, the only person who’ll care is you. 
Nobody else will even notice. 

And even if it does well, LinkedIn will remember it for… at most a week. 

(Is anyone still talking about that romantic Coldplay concert drama? Nope. Already forgotten.) 

So don’t glorify your content too much. 

Post what’s on your mind right now. 
Be authentic. 

It’s the fastest way to test an idea, and maybe even go viral. 

🫳🎤
…more

content:
You don’t have to spend hours creating content (no, this is not a product pitch, even if it sounds like one). Read this if you want to make content successful: 

– Yesterday, I posted 2 sentences with a native graphic on LinkedIn. (107 profile visits, 17 new followers, 8k+ impressions)
– A few days ago, Stephen Smith posted a screenshot of a LinkedIn notification. 
– Karen Kluss 🍬 shared a screenshot of LinkedIn comments with a single word, and it performed insanely well. "Exactly." 

That’s it. 
No overthinking. 
No long-form essays. 

Why does this work? 

Because LinkedIn-native UI/UX screenshots naturally grab attention. 

People are curious. ⤵
They’re interested in patterns and what actually works on this platform. 

The only real rule is this: 

⚠️ You need to deliver value – and “value” can mean many things:
– useful 
– educational 
– entertaining 

There’s no need to overengineer your posts if you know how to express the idea as simply and clearly as possible. 

P.S. This post didn’t take me much time either.
…more

content:
There are 2 potential reasons why you might be losing additional engagement on your posts: 

1. You’re not actively replying to comments on your posts. 

I know being a “reply guy” can be time-consuming, but community management has its purpose. Besides building relationships, your commenting activity also appears in your connections’ feeds. 

2. The second reason is more of a LinkedIn issue. 

LinkedIn doesn’t always send notifications for new comments on older posts and tends to focus only on comments under recent posts. 

I noticed this by accident when I checked the activity on my older posts and realised that some comments hadn’t been answered – even though I never received any notifications about them. 

Remember: the more you engage in the comments, the more a post’s activity and longevity are refreshed. 

So what do I do? 

At the end of each week, I go back to posts from the past week and reply to any new comments that appeared. 

👉 PRO TIP: Sort comments by “Most recent.” 

And even if it’s the weekend, try to stay active. 

The algorithm doesn’t really care about days of the week. 

Sometimes, even a weekend post can go viral – especially if you engage a bit.
…more

content:
Marketers are a bit like voyeurs. And as perverse as that may sound, here’s the brutal truth: 

If you wanna create truly great content, you have to become someone else entirely. 

→ You have to adopt someone else’s habits. 
→ You have to think like your target audience. 
→ You have to step into your ICP’s shoes and instinctively know what they’ll like. 

Wearing many hats doesn’t just mean switching between clients’ social media accounts or juggling multiple roles and tasks in a business. 

It means changing your character masks. 

Is it a bit psycho and unsettling? 

Yes, it is. But honestly, I didn’t expect anything different from a marketer’s job.
It’s a wild ride. 

TL;DR: You have to be a little crazy to do marketing. Otherwise, marketing will make you crazy.
…more

content:
Is going viral on LinkedIn impossible? Yes... unless you know how to help it a little. I know a few more steps that can help you to be visible. Breakdown: 

BTW, if you find this post useful, feel free to save it or repost it so you can find it later in your bookmarks or profile history. 

And now: 

I’ve grouped the LinkedIn algorithm-triggering signals into 3 categories: 

🟢 Strong signals 
🟡 Medium signals 
🔴 Weaker signals 

These are the actions that help your post move to the top of the feed. 

----------

🟢 Strong signals (highest weight) 
1. “Read more” click
Expanding the text = a clear signal of interest. 

2. Dwell time (time spent on the post) 
When someone stops scrolling and reads or watches. 
Most common with carousels, videos, and infographics. 

3. Comments (especially meaningful ones) 
Not “🔥” or auto-comments, but full sentences. 
The algorithm can tell the difference. 

4. Author replies in the comments 
Every reply extends the post’s lifespan. 

----------

🟡 Medium signals 

5. Opening the comments section 
It signals: “I want to see the discussion.” 

6. Shares / Reposts 
Especially with added text – this shows higher intent. 

7. Saving the post 
A very strong, but silent signal that someone wants to come back to it. 

----------

🔴 Weaker signals (still important) 

8. Reactions (like, celebrate, insightful, etc.) 
Less weight than comments, but they still help. 

9. Clicking on the author’s profile 
Signals trust or curiosity. 

10. Following after interacting with a post 
A very strong downstream signal. 

----------

And since LinkedIn hasn’t been very kind to me lately (I’ve been warned about account restrictions more than once and got banned for a while 😂), here are a few things LinkedIn does NOT like: 

– Fast scrolling without stopping 
– Clicking → immediately going back 
– One-word, low-context comments 
– Editing a post after publishing 
– View multiple profiles at once 

Hopefully, my mistakes will be your lessons and help you get more of the attention you’re aiming for. 

Don’t forget to save or repost.

content:
Thank you, LinkedIn, for bringing drama into my life so I have content to write about. Yesterday, my account got restricted. But do you know what happened? 

I shared what happened in a single post: just a screenshot and plain text. 

Result? 

20k+ impressions. 

I hadn’t seen numbers like this in almost three weeks. 

One nerve-wracking LinkedIn moment, delivered in 24 hours: 
– 26,494 impressions 
– 235 profile views 
– 10 new followers 
– 102 reactions 
– 278 comments 
– 3 reposts 
– 18 saves 
– 2 shares via LinkedIn messages 

And the stats are still climbing. 

Sometimes I struggle to find topics. Then LinkedIn steps into my life (positively or negatively) and hands me a piece of content that performs. 

It practically writes itself. 

TL;DR: LinkedIn is still the biggest topic on LinkedIn.

content:
I had a mini-heart attack this morning. LinkedIn restricted me, and I stopped existing for a few minutes. What happened? 

LinkedIn accused me of being automated or using some "unauthorised" tools and extensions. 

Which is not true, I have 3 plugins installed that I don't use, but I deleted them just to be safe. 

What could be causing this situation? 

1. Automation tools (for engagement, DMs, scraping) 
2. Excessive connection requests 
3. Rapid-fire actions ("being too fast" in activities) 
4. Copy-paste behaviour (e.g. same DMs sent to multiple contacts) 
5. Account hopping & Suspicious logins 
6. Aggressive outbound messaging 
7. Third-party browser extensions 

Solution? 

Avoid the above points. 🙃

Other things that can help significantly: 

– buy Premium (some quotas will be freed up) 
– make connections with LinkedIn employees 
– create a backup account and grow it simultaneously with the main account (but who would have time for that, right?) 

This is also a way to get your adrenaline pumping during the weekend. 

Hopefully, your time here is less terrifying!

content:
To create a quite successful post on LinkedIn, you don't need to write long essays. Often, all you need is "just" this:

Write 1 to 3 sentences, each on its own line. 

The trick is knowing which 3 sentences to write. 😅 

It also helps to: 
– add a compelling visual (sometimes a screenshot or a meme is enough), 
– write the first comment (you need to train people to visit the comments section). 

My last 3-line post brought me at least 16 new followers. 

Three lines that can make a difference. 

Don’t waste the potential of short text posts. 
Study what works for others and learn how to work with text. 

TL;DR: Express the idea clearly and as simply as possible.

content:
Not getting attention for your posts? Let me tell you what you’re doing wrong, and what people actually pay attention to: 

We live in a short-attention span economy. 

We want the whole point in a 15-second video, and not everyone can handle reading long texts anymore (please, start reading books to fix this!). 

So, what don’t people read? 

• Text that’s too long without paragraphs 
• Long text about uninteresting topics 
• AI-generated text that feels… synthetic 
• Long introductions without a clear point or a takeaway for the reader 

What do people prefer to read? 

• Shorter text with paragraphs and bullet points 
• Simple language (“explain it like I’m 5”) 
• Real experiences, clear opinions, facts, and emotions 
• Text that’s accompanied by an image (which could carry meaning on its own) 

Exception for long text: If you’re a highly respected and well-known person, you can write long posts – people will savour every single word you write. 

TL;DR: 
Length, context, content, and the way you present it all matter.

content:
An updated version of the Benjamin Franklin quote: "The only certainties in life are dea*h, paying taxes, and being deleted by LinkedIn." AKA: 

LinkedIn may have at least two reasons to remove you from the platform. 

1. When you post too much 
2. When you don’t post at all 

1) In the first case, it can happen when LinkedIn sees strange behaviour patterns and assumes you’re using automation, for example: 
 – commenting excessively 
 – sending a lot of connection requests 
 – sending a lot of messages 
 – repeatedly using certain (maybe even inappropriate) phrases 
 – using third-party tools, scheduling apps, etc. 

2) In the second case, it can be about zero activity, a completely inactive account that hasn’t moved a finger in a long time. 

Sometimes I don’t understand this platform, but I guess not everything has to make perfect sense. 

Just think of one important thing: 

LinkedIn punishes extremes and rewards consistency.

❌ But the wrong size of the text

LinkedIn almost deleted my account. I was wrongly flagged for something I basically didn’t do, but I understand what happened: 

LinkedIn assumed I was using 3rd-party tools or automation to manage my profile. 

Spoiler alert: I wasn’t. I was just unusually active that day: 😅
• I generated 150+ comments on my posts because I led dialogue.
• I replied in bulk to roughly 30+ messages over the course of a month. 

This worried LinkedIn, and the next day, I woke up and realised that LI automatically kicked me out. I had to log in manually and was greeted with an unpleasant warning. 

My 2 overall recommendations are: 
1) Don’t use tools for massive bot-like commenting – you’re not helping anyone; you’re only putting yourself at risk. 
2. Watch your limits, because excessive commenting in a short period can trigger temporary restrictions. 

Hope you had a better mid-week! 😃

Social media is about being social – about interacting. (Which is why I still don’t understand why so many try to fake it or shortcut it with AI.) 

I believe more than 50% of success comes from active commenting. 

By actively commenting, you can: 
– Connect with interesting people you can actually chat with 
– Discover potential clients, customers, and coworkers
– Expand your knowledge 
– Build your reputation 
– Create material for future posts 
– Remember people better (Hi, Rana) 😉

content:
350 followers, but over 235k impressions. I usually don’t share client case studies, but today I’ll make an exception and show you what worked: 

Over the summer, a local association approached me to help with their annual International Theatre Festival, which runs for one week each October. 

🎯 Goal: Build awareness on social media (Instagram) and (in)directly support ticket sales for the artists’ performances. 

We started with roughly 300 followers in August and only 8,000 impressions over the previous three months (July – September). 

Active account management began in the last week of August, continued through September, and covered the first three weeks of October. 

By the end, followers had grown slightly to 355, but impressions skyrocketed to 235k+ over August – October. 

⚠️ What helped? 
• Daily posts (+ Stories). 
• Inviting performers as co-creators, which exposed content to their fans; some actors were quite well-known. 
• A MEME post – one actor sent photos from previous performances; I spotted a funny similarity to a Drake MEME. This post alone reached 17k+ impressions. 
• Actively commenting and creating dialogue with followers. 
• Highlighting the festival’s 31-year history, despite Instagram's presence only starting in the last 2 – 3 years. 
• Media coverage (radio, TV, newspapers). 
• Boosted posts – paid ads focused on ticket sales and driving traffic to the webshop, rather than post engagement. 
• Tagging relevant people, sponsors, and institutions. 
• A “peak period” leading up to the festival: posting video invites by actors as Reels, sometimes publishing 5+ posts per day. 

But this isn’t just about impressions – we sold out several of the 23 performances, and the remaining shows still had very high attendance. 

It was all a team effort, but there were only two people who were most involved in this: Me and Patrik, who was part of the organising team. 

I wanted to publish this at the end of October, but somehow I forgot about it; better late than never. 😅 

TL;DR: Work on multiple projects, it will give you valuable experience and a nice collection for your portfolio.

Many of us think LinkedIn isn’t worth posting on during the weekend because “no one is here.” What if I told you we’re wrong? 

Yes, LinkedIn isn’t at its strongest on weekends, but people are here. 

Want proof? 

Switch your main feed from “Most relevant” to “Most recent.” 

You’ll see it immediately: 
– every minute someone likes something, 
– someone comments on something, 
– someone posts something. 

LinkedIn has 1.2B+ users, so there’s always someone here, even on a Sunday. 

Keep contributing. 
Don’t underestimate the growth of this platform. 

You’ll be grateful you didn’t give up. 

Happy weekend!

content:
More than half of my LinkedIn posts would probably fail and get very poor reach if I didn’t do one thing that helps scale them. That thing is: 

Active commenting 

You might have noticed that some of my posts have more comments than reactions. That’s not an accident. 

Here’s what I do: 

1. I always leave the first comment under my own post. It already counts as engagement and gives people a direction to respond. 
2. When someone comments, I reply and try to turn it into a real conversation, not a one-word response. 
3. People remember you much more from conversations than from likes. 
4. From my perspective as a creator, comments have a different impact: each one triggers its own notification, while likes are bundled together. That makes comments easier to engage with and harder to overlook. 
5. Often, people enjoy browsing the comment section even more than the post itself. 

To achieve better results on LinkedIn, don’t just post. 
Stick around and talk to people. 

TL;DR: Comments > Reactions

content:
Oops, I did it again. I turned a viral comment into a quite successful post. It just always takes a few changes: 

What's great about LinkedIn is that funny content thrives here and can get a significant response in the comments. 

If you see above-average results for your comment, it's worth trying to make it a main post. 

However, there are a few things you usually need to consider (and apply), for example: 
→ Add context and expand the written part. 
→ Ideally, include a funny image – and if it doesn’t fully match the text, add extra context so it makes sense. 
→ Keep it very short, so the original idea doesn’t get “lost” when turned into a separate post. 

What you saw yesterday was the original comment I left under a post by Michelle Thomas. The comment received 37 likes and 9.6k+ impressions. 

In the form of a slightly edited post, it reached: 
139 likes, 11k impressions, 27 profile views, and 6 followers. 

TL;DR: Engage in the comment sections of people who are already creating viral posts. It’s a prerequisite for turning a successful comment into a successful post.

content:
I partially disappeared from LinkedIn for two weeks and watched what would happen:

No, I didn't delete my account; I just limited the time I spent there because I decided to travel across Europe for 2 weeks.

Fortunately, I managed to maintain, and in some cases, increase the number of weekly impressions.

How?

• I prepared posts in advance for 15 days (text + images)
• It was educational content and funny content (in a ratio of 7 to 8)
• 6 posts were purely text, and 9 also had an image
• I also prepared the first comment in advance, which I always include under the post
• I published it early in the morning (approximately between 7 and 9 am)
• I always replied to all posts in the evening

Despite the fact that I reduced my weekly time on LinkedIn from 7 hours and 30 minutes to 3 hours and 43 minutes, I managed to have over 41k weekly impressions.

TL;DR or Moral of the story:

Even if you are travelling, with enough preparation and allocated time, you can enjoy life and still achieve relatively good results on this platform. The point is to stay active and show up every single day.

content:
That feeling when you’ve changed so much that even LinkedIn doesn’t recognise you anymore. 😀 But that’s okay, because… 

If you’re a marketer, you have to “wear a lot of hats.” 

Which means creating different personas online – and, with them, new psychological profiles. 

On social media, you adapt: 
• your vocabulary 
• your way of expressing yourself 
• your tone 
• even your mindset 

Is this faking authenticity? 

Partially, yes. But social media works 99% of the time by sharing only what we want to share. 

The result? Attracting the right audience. 

If you can play this psychological game well, the results can be excellent. 

So don’t be surprised if you see me speaking in a style you’re not used to – you might not even recognise me. 

TL;DR: Don’t believe everything you see online. It’s all a game of how we simulate the personalities we want you to see.

content:
Now I’m going to tell you something that might hurt, but the sooner you realise it, the easier it will be for you in the future: 

In the eyes of corporations (and maybe some people), you’re just another number, not worth a reply. 

When I finished university, I sent out 5 resumes with cover letters, expecting companies to fight over me. 

LOL! 

Not a single response. 

“How is that possible? I have a degree!” 

That’s always the illusion shared by the other 100 applicants at the same time. 

Now imagine a well-paid remote position with over 1,000 applicants from all over the world. 

You don’t even stand a chance of a recruiter seeing your application.
Time, attention, and every possible resource are being saved. 

THE ONLY WAY TO STAND OUT IS TO WORK ON YOUR OWN PROJECTS AND BUILD A PROFILE THAT MAKES PEOPLE COME TO YOU. 

Once you understand this, you’ll stop feeling disappointed by rejections and being ignored by companies. 

TL;DR: Your (net)worth grows with your self-made projects. Stop waiting for replies. Build your own path, and people will come to you.

content:
Today, while casually scrolling through Product Hunt, I came across a post by 🦄 Nika Kotláriková that made me stop for a moment.

She wrote about how, years ago, she would delete a post the second it didn’t get enough reactions not because it was bad, but because she was scared people would see it as a failure. And then she said something so simple and honest: when a post doesn’t perform well, almost nobody notices it. The only person who feels the weight of low engagement is usually the one who posted it.

That felt uncomfortably true.

So many of us post with hope, refresh the screen a few times, and quietly feel disappointed when the numbers don’t move. We start questioning the content, our skills, sometimes even ourselves. But her perspective reframed it for me. Low-performing content isn’t failure it’s practice. It’s learning. It’s part of showing up.

What really stayed with me was this idea of not deleting. Let the post exist. Let it teach you something. Let it sit there quietly, doing its job, even if that job isn’t applause.

This post was such a gentle reminder that growth doesn’t always look like engagement. Sometimes it just looks like consistency, patience, and continuing anyway.

Now I’m going to tell you something that might hurt, but the sooner you realise it, the easier it will be for you in the future: 

In the eyes of corporations (and maybe some people), you’re just another number, not worth a reply. 

When I finished university, I sent out 5 resumes with cover letters, expecting companies to fight over me. 

LOL! 

Not a single response. 

“How is that possible? I have a degree!” 

That’s always the illusion shared by the other 100 applicants at the same time. 

Now imagine a well-paid remote position with over 1,000 applicants from all over the world. 

You don’t even stand a chance of a recruiter seeing your application.
Time, attention, and every possible resource are being saved. 

THE ONLY WAY TO STAND OUT IS TO WORK ON YOUR OWN PROJECTS AND BUILD A PROFILE THAT MAKES PEOPLE COME TO YOU. 

Once you understand this, you’ll stop feeling disappointed by rejections and being ignored by companies. 

TL;DR: Your (net)worth grows with your self-made projects. Stop waiting for replies. Build your own path, and people will come to you.

It depends on things like: 
 – the quality of production 
– the idea you’re presenting 
– your on-camera performance 

My recommendation? 

Experiment with video and draw inspiration from creators who do it well.
For me, that’s creators like Jenna Sipponen, Louis Butterfield, and Margaret Sikora. 

I know videos require a lot of energy, but if you approach them with a well-thought-out concept and follow the points above, your effort can lead to very satisfying results.

content:
Most of my posts usually went viral on one specific day. That day is: (drumroll, please) 

Thursday. 

Why? 

I don’t have any scientific data to back this up, so this is purely my own theory. 

1. Have you ever heard the saying, “Thursday is the little Friday”? 

People on LinkedIn are less active during the weekend, and since Friday already feels like the weekend, engagement isn’t great then either. 

That’s why Thursday is the last day when people still come to a career-focused platform to catch up on what happened during the workweek. 

(Monday isn’t ideal for posting either – most people are busy with meetings and planning.) 

2. And one more: 

Thursday is when most B2B newsletters, reports, and corporate updates are published. 

So people are already in a “content consumption mode.” 

But my general recommendation is simple: 

Post every day, because consistent effort has a cumulative effect that leads to success. You never know which day will be your breakthrough.

content:
There are three ways to go viral on LinkedIn. The 2nd one is completely new to me, and at least one of these methods can be fully controlled. 

1. Friend activity 
If someone in my connections interacts with my post, my content appears in the feed of their connections. That’s how it spreads from my 1st-degree connections to 2nd- and 3rd-degree connections. 

You can influence this by: 
• following trends and creating engaging content 
• asking friends (1st-degree connections) to interact with your posts 

2. Your content appears in someone’s feed as “suggested” (by LinkedIn) 
This type of feed doesn’t depend on whether your friends (1st-degree connections) interacted. LinkedIn shows these posts to other people based on its own algorithm. I actually posted about this type of feed yesterday. 

You cannot directly control this. These are posts from outside your network that LinkedIn thinks you might find valuable based on specific signals. 

3. Paid boost (ads) 
If you create content, you can promote it to a selected target audience through paid campaigns. 

You can control this by: 
• creating content that has a chance to be popular based on past performance 
• managing your budget and targeting parameters 

The truth is: you don’t need to control all three paths. 

Master the one you can influence, and the algorithm will eventually reward you. 

Consistency always beats virality.

content:
LinkedIn has started showing me posts from strangers out of nowhere in my feed as "Suggested". What does this mean? 

🟠 From LinkedIn’s perspective: 

It’s one of the ways to increase engagement on the platform, connect people, and encourage them to spend more time here. 

More time on the platform = more attractive advertising space for marketers = revenue for LinkedIn. 

🟠 From a user’s perspective: 
It’s a way to expand our (non)professional circles and earn some likes from strangers, which, let’s be honest, gives our ego a little boost. 

How does it work?

Interestingly, a suggested post doesn’t need to be liked by anyone in my 1st-degree connections to appear in my feed. 

If I, as a 2nd- or 3rd-degree connection, like it, it increases that user’s chances of being featured to others (my connections and a wider audience)… 

And just like that, a post has the potential to GO VIRAL. 

Have you noticed this happening in your feed too?

content:
The ultimate hack to manage unsolicited pitches politely without losing your sanity: 

Okay, I have to admit – I don’t have this fully mastered. 

When someone pitches me a product I’m not interested in, I usually just say: 

“No, thanks.” 

If they keep pitching, I will try a more refined approach. My message looks like this: 

“Thank you, [XY], I appreciate your offer. At the moment, this isn’t relevant for my purpose, but I’ll keep your contact and reach out if I ever need it.” 

If they continue pitching even after that, I simply stop opening the messages. 

Time, attention, and energy cost something, and my job is to save these commodities for both parties. 

TL;DR: Be polite, but use the most valuable things you have wisely.

content:
How to grow on social media faster without significant effort? I have one bad and one good news. The bad one: 

You can’t... 
You can’t simply grow without putting in the work. 

The good news: 
You can grow by taking certain steps every day. 

The steps to grow are: 
• post valuable content once a day (challenge: 2–3×) 
• actively comment posts from at least 5 large creators with high engagement 
• actively comment posts from smaller creators as well (also at least 5) 
• engage under your own posts and reply to every comment 
• add people to your network who interact with similar content + your ICP 

Additionally: 
• optimize your profile 
• tag people only when it adds context 
• collaborate with another creator on a joint post 
• publish a long-form article once a month (for better search visibility) 
• meet someone new (send 1–3 quality DMs a day) – even just a greeting 

TL;DR: Results come with effort. Start taking the right steps today.

content:
Why does it feel like you have to lie on your CV just to land a job? Well, it's maybe because 

Companies have wildly exaggerated expectations. 

They're literally looking for a Superman in Gotham City to rebuild it into Metropolis. 

They want: 
• someone who wears many hats 
• someone flexible and adaptable to constant change 
• someone who can move fast in a “fast-paced environment” 
• someone who takes ownership and thinks like a CEO 

Please. 😀

If someone truly has the mindset of a CEO, they’re not applying for a job. 

They’re building their own company. 

And what do companies offer in return? 
• a “competitive salary” 
• the classic “we’re a family” line 
• dynamic team 
• opportunity to make a real difference in the industry (OK, LOL)

Companies demand extreme specificity from people, but offer very abstract terms. 

IS IT POSSIBLE TO GET A JOB WITHOUT FAKING OUR AUTHENTICITY AT ALL? 

Maybe the problem isn’t that candidates exaggerate. 
Maybe it’s that honesty no longer fits job descriptions. 

The best thing I could do was focus on myself, letting those "opportunities" go, and people started chasing me. 

Now I see why so many people choose to build their own businesses instead of bargaining their authenticity for vague offers. 

The end of the rage.
…more

content:
Do you know which type of content will lose the game on LinkedIn in the long run? 

The one created by artificial intelligence. 

I’ve looked at several profiles where every post is copy-paste AI-generated content, and those posts don’t perform well. 

Why? 
• These “AI contributors” usually don’t build a community, actively comment, or create real dialogue. 
• AI content is inauthentic and impersonal – it only replicates. 
• It doesn't capture real experience or real lessons learned. 

Over time, synthetic AI content will become generic and run-of-the-mill. 

Take inspiration from people who create authentically, for example… 
Iuliia Shnai, Josie Geistfeld 👋, Michał Cieślak, Paulina Hryniewicz or Sandra Đajic, to name a few. 

TL;DR: Don’t overdo it with AI.

content:
One of the most effective ways to grab attention and go viral is something many people are not daring to do: 

👉 Make a joke out of a pain point (caused by something or someone). 

Which can sometimes be personal, controversial, or even a little insulting. 

BUT 

People relate because they’ve felt the same frustration. 
So they understand the insider joke as it applies to them as well. 

The liquid glass for iOS is just one example. Others include:

– Designers being told, “Make it pop!” 
– LinkedIn showing the useless note: "Your profile appeared in search 91 times." 
– Trying to stay incognito on WhatsApp by deleting messages… but the other person still sees “Message deleted.” 

Turn shared annoyances into jokes, and suddenly your content resonates. 

TL;DR: Relatable pain = Humor = Engagement.

It is simple like that.

content:
What is the best way to validate a product? Many say, "By putting it behind a paywall." Eehh... not really. 

– How do you get an initial group of users who help build trust and reputation? 
– How do you get test users who give you feedback you can actually fix? 
– How can you improve the product without real feedback? 
– And how do you stay competitive without continuous improvements? 

I’m not saying everything should be free, but a trial version does have a purpose. 

It creates a space where the user builds a relationship with your product before you ask them for anything. 

And if the product is truly good, a trial isn’t a loss. 
It's the cheapest marketing you can afford. 

Example: 

I used CapCut for 3 for free. When they switched to a paid version, I didn’t feel like searching for a new tool and learning it from scratch, so I paid. If they had done that after a single trial week, they would’ve gotten nothing from me. 

TL;DR: Have trial versions.
…more

content:
Does cross-posting between X and LinkedIn actually work? Or should you adapt the content first? I’ve got this question and have an answer for you:

It depends. 😃 

Okay, now seriously. 

I don’t recommend literal cross-posting, and here’s why:
• Topics suited for LinkedIn may not be relevant for X. 
• People on LinkedIn and X communicate and express ideas differently.
• Certain formats aren’t supported on both platforms (for example, X doesn’t allow PDF documents or carousels). 
• X has a character limit unless you have a Premium account, so you’d need to adjust the wording anyway. 

Of course, there are always exceptions, for example, major trending topics that spread across both platforms. 

A good example is the Coldplay cheating couple. 

The situation became a global entertainment moment, but it also leaked into the professional space on LinkedIn because the cheating duo were well-known industry figures. 

Either way, even if you find a topic relevant for both platforms, I still recommend adapting your posts separately. 

TL;DR: Don’t cross-post blindly, customise your content.

content:
Delete or not delete the post that doesn’t take off? Keep reading because my experience from yesterday can save you. 

The short answer is: NO.

But let me explain it:

Yesterday, I posted something during lunch. In the first crucial hour, the post had barely 200 impressions and 4 or 5 likes (one of them was mine).

→ But 3 hours later, the post had 3,211 impressions, 25 likes, 24 comments, and 4 new followers.

→ 24 hours later: 18,286 impressions, 60 profile views, 47 likes, 59 comments, and 19 followers.

What could have helped?

I quoted and tagged Daniel R. Hires 🌍, who shared his POV under my post. His engagement likely pushed the post to his 17k+ audience.

Don’t delete posts that don’t take off, because:
– It’s practice.
– You can see what doesn’t work and what does.
– It still can earn a few impressions, and you won’t skip the day.
 – Posts can take off later.

TL;DR: Posts sometimes grow slowly. Don’t make destructive decisions too soon.
…more

content:
I was approached for a collaboration by a company valued at over $2 billion, with more than $200M in annual revenue. Their offer sounded ridiculous: 

25% revenue payout from the affiliate for paid subscription conversions in a 7-day timeframe. 

Is it technically fair? 
Yes, it is BUT – there's a huge BUT. 

This is not how marketing works. 

⚠️ Affiliate partnerships work best when the promoter is a long-term user aligned with the product’s vision, making it easier to sell authentically to the audience. 

Otherwise, you end up promoting a company for free to an international community you’ve been building for over 3 years. 

If a company of this size cannot afford marketers who understand this core principle, I am quite concerned about their approach and practices.

content:
Please, people of LinkedIn… I’m begging you, please. Just stop doing these things: 

• Offering me a job that requires relocation to a “cost-efficient” country just so you can pay me less than I already make. 
• Inviting me to an exploratory call for a role and then ghosting me like it never happened. 
• Sending connection requests only to pitch a product I’m not even the target audience for. 
• Asking me to complete an assignment, using the output for your own purposes, and then months later telling me you “moved forward with someone else.” 

Now, I finally understand why people started treating LinkedIn more as a cringe platform rather than a career platform. 

It is one big joke.

content:
What should you post on LinkedIn if you want to succeed? There’s one principle that is the most basic and important: 

👉 Authenticity. 

❌ Don’t share generic content (e.g., “What is marketing?”) 
✅ Share personal experience (e.g., your results, process, progress, lessons) 

2 types of posts will always perform well on LinkedIn:
1. Content about LinkedIn – because the platform is at the centre of interest.
2. Content about success and the lessons to achieve it. 

People want to feel in control of their lives and their careers. They want to succeed, so they naturally follow people who show them how to get there.

Everyone wants to be a winner. 
Nobody wants to be a loser.

That’s why people learn from those ahead of them.
And learning requires skill, repetition, and practice.

So take a piece of paper, take a pen, open a text editor and start writing.

TL;DR: Success comes from consistent practice and learning from the best.

content:
My last 2–3 posts underperformed and got significantly fewer impressions compared to others. So, what happened? One thing:

I posted in the evening, when many of my followers were either asleep or not active on LinkedIn.

This means that during the crucial first hour after publishing, most people couldn’t see my post.

As a result, it didn’t get the momentum to spread further based on engagement.

🟠 What you can do to avoid this: 
– Post when most of your audience is active.
– Repost at a time when you can still reach the majority of your audience.

🟠 One interesting finding: 
– MEMEs received more engagement than "regular posts" with graphs despite having fewer impressions.

I’ve left a slightly motivational note in the comments.

content:
What did one month of consistent LinkedIn posting bring me? More than I hoped for, and I share the results happily with you (including the financial side). 😉 

Around mid-September, I started posting every single day (sometimes even twice a day). 

In October, I had a few short breaks, either due to health conditions or letting certain posts “trend” a bit longer. 

Still, despite those small pauses, my one month of consistent activity brought me (during October): 
• over 150K impressions 
• recognition from LinkedIn Top Voices and big creators (who started commenting on my posts) 
• 500+ new connections 
• 3 sponsors, with a total deal value of around $1.3K 
• 3 job offers (which I turned down, they just weren’t the right fit) 

All of this is possible on this platform. 

And I don’t even pay for LinkedIn Premium! 

Is there any post you’re proud of from the past month? Feel free to share it below.
…more

content:
I used to have 100,000 views a week on LinkedIn, and then dropped to 14,000. Algorithm? No... something else.

I stopped posting 2 posts a day regularly. I lost track due to health issues, and now I need to get back on track.

Quick tips to achieve this (not only) with content:

• Eye-catching image.

content:
It’s okay to turn down a partnership offer. Even a paid one from a top company that has raised $25M+. It really happened like this:

I always appreciate it when someone thinks of me for a paid partnership; it feels so good. 😀

But you don’t have to take a deal just because it’s paid.

It’s in everyone’s best interest to stand up for your values ​​and want the best for not only yourself and your audience, but also the company that requires the partnership.

My approach is simple:

1. Say thank you.
2. Politely explain that you’re not the best fit.
3. You can offer your own perspective, e.g. alternatives that would help the company.
4. Thank them again for their trust.

You’ll save time and resources on both sides.

TL;DR: Accept the offers that truly align with you. 

For sure, save this template on how to be polite and friendly. 😉

TL;DR: Try to catch the attention of LinkedIn Top Voices and stay close to them.

content:
Almost the same LinkedIn post, but different results. 29,900 impressions vs. 3,341 impressions; 480 likes vs. 80 likes. How is it possible? I will explain: 👇 

I tried posting on LinkedIn the same content that went viral a week earlier. The result? It didn’t go viral – only semi-viral.

Here’s why I think that happened, ranked from most to least likely:

1️⃣ In the first post, I used a MEME image (an attention-grabber).

2️⃣ The first post went out during the workweek when traffic is higher; the second one was posted on the weekend.

3️⃣ I gave the first post more “space” to stand out – I didn’t post or repost anything else that day. In the second case, I reposted something else on the same day, which may have divided the attention of the audience.

4️⃣ The first post benefited from previous activity and previous viral post – people visited my profile more, interacted, and engaged with my posts. The second post didn’t have that momentum.

5️⃣ I repeated myself – it’s possible that publishing the same text content repeatedly makes it feel less original, reducing engagement.

TL;DR: Timing, context, and format matter.

PRO TIP: Be engaging and try to answer all comments.

content:
I ran a LinkedIn posting experiment, and I need to say, I’m more than happy with the results. What was the point, and what did I learn? Transparent results: 

• I posted on LinkedIn every day for 7 days straight, twice a day, including weekends (until now, I was more of an “occasional” poster).
• Less than two weeks ago, I had a viral post, so this week looked like a “drop” in comparison.

BUT…

👍 Most of my posts during this period reached 1K+ impressions, some even 2K+, 3K+, or 4K+.
👍 The impressions seemed to grow with every new post.
👍 Engagement increased too (around 20 reactions on average) – commenting and keeping discussions going definitely helps.
👍 My profile views, followers, and connections went up (I gained at least 50 new ones. I am not kidding, one post brought me 10 new connections).

👉 Memes and celebratory posts performed the best.

And now that I’ve gotten used to seeing these positive results, I actually feel like continuing.

Should I? 😄

I ran a LinkedIn posting experiment, and I need to say, I’m more than happy with the results. What was the point, and what did I learn? Transparent results: 

• I posted on LinkedIn every day for 7 days straight, twice a day, including weekends (until now, I was more of an “occasional” poster).
• Less than two weeks ago, I had a viral post, so this week looked like a “drop” in comparison.

BUT…

👍 Most of my posts during this period reached 1K+ impressions, some even 2K+, 3K+, or 4K+.
👍 The impressions seemed to grow with every new post.
👍 Engagement increased too (around 20 reactions on average) – commenting and keeping discussions going definitely helps.
👍 My profile views, followers, and connections went up (I gained at least 50 new ones. I am not kidding, one post brought me 10 new connections).

👉 Memes and celebratory posts performed the best.

And now that I’ve gotten used to seeing these positive results, I actually feel like continuing.

Should I? 😄
…more
